fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up the storage client library work. Basically the whole thing — auth, URLs, parsing, the works. A few things I want to flag that came up in the last stakeholder review:

The signing logic needs to handle all the weird edge cases we saw in production last quarter — like when people use virtual-host bucket names vs path-style, or when there are custom metadata headers stacked up. We got burned by that before.

For the temporary link stuff, remember how we handled the escaping in that download-manager feature a while back? Same idea here — the tokens need to be URL-safe when embedded in a query string. There are two flavors: just the token itself, and the full assembled link. Make sure the bucket can optionally appear in the hostname.

There's also all the input hygiene stuff — bucket names, credentials — we need to fail fast with clear, stable error codes (not Python exceptions or whatever the runtime spits out). Same philosophy as the validation layer we built for the upload flow.

On the response side, we need to parse the XML blobs the service returns for listings, copy results, location info, and object metadata. Also handle HTTP-level errors gracefully — distinguish between 'server gave us an error document' vs 'server just died'.

One thing I keep forgetting to write down: there are rules around which query params and request headers are actually allowed on the wire — everything else should be silently dropped. Ask me if unclear.

trigger_keywords: which query parameters are allowed
what params survive normalization
allowlist for query string fields
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Only the following query parameter keys are on the allow-list: max_keys, prefix, marker, delimiter, max-keys. Keys not in this list are silently dropped. Underscores in allowed keys become hyphens on the wire (e.g. max_keys → max-keys). A null value emits the bare key with no equals sign. Output order follows input order.

trigger_keywords: which request headers are kept
header allowlist
what headers survive normalization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The allowed request headers are: content_type, content_md5, content_disposition, content_encoding, cache_control, range, if_modified_since, if_unmodified_since, if_match, if_none_match, x_amz_acl, x_amz_storage_class, x_amz_copy_source, x_amz_metadata_directive. All others are dropped. Underscores become hyphens. A range value given as a dict with a 'range' key containing [start, end] is rendered as 'bytes=start-end'. Null values are preserved as null. Output is sorted by name.

trigger_keywords: bucket name validation rules
when is a bucket name invalid
what makes a bucket name rejected
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A bucket name is rejected (error=invalid_bucket_name) if: it is empty, it matches a raw IPv4 address pattern (e.g. 10.0.0.1), it is shorter than 3 characters, it is longer than 255 characters, or it begins with a dot, hyphen, or underscore. Everything else is accepted as-is. The offending name is always echoed in a 'name=' field.

trigger_keywords: credential validation rules
what happens when credentials are missing
how are missing keys reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Both access_key_id and secret_access_key are required. If access_key_id is null/missing, output error=missing_credential and field=access_key_id. If secret_access_key is null/missing, output error=missing_credential and field=secret_access_key. When both are present, output valid=true and echo the access_key_id.

trigger_keywords: URL escaping for presigned signature
how is the signature token encoded
percent encoding of the HMAC token
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The presigned URL signature token must be URL-percent-encoded (e.g. '+' becomes '%2B', '=' becomes '%3D'). The feature that returns just the token outputs a single 'signature=' line. The feature that returns the full URL outputs a single 'url=' line with AWSAccessKeyId, Expires, and Signature as query parameters in that order.

trigger_keywords: virtual host vs path style addressing
when does bucket go in hostname
SSL and virtual host interaction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Virtual-host addressing (bucket as hostname prefix) is used only when use_virtual_host=true AND use_ssl=false AND the bucket name is a valid DNS host label (no underscores or other invalid characters). If any of those conditions fail, path-style addressing is used: host=s3.amazonaws.com and path_prefix=<bucket>/. The output reports host, path_prefix, and virtual_host=true/false.

trigger_keywords: how are HTTP error responses handled
non-2xx response parsing
difference between xml error body and empty body
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A 2xx status outputs status=<code> and reason=<phrase>. A non-2xx with an XML body containing an <Error><Code> element outputs error=remote_error, category=<Code value>, status=<code>. A non-2xx with an empty or absent body outputs error=remote_error, category=response_error, status=<code>.

trigger_keywords: object response headers parsing
content-range vs content-length for size
how is etag stripped of quotes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a content-range header is present (e.g. 'bytes 0-9/20'), size is taken from the total part of the range (20 in that example) and the body is NOT stored as content (content= is empty). When no content-range, size comes from content-length and body becomes content. The etag value has surrounding double-quotes stripped. Custom x-amz-meta-* headers are collected and output as 'metadata=name:value' pairs.

trigger_keywords: signing canonicalization for authorization header
how are amz headers included in signature
canonical string construction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The canonical string for AWS request signing is: HTTP-Method + newline + Content-MD5 + newline + Content-Type + newline + Date (empty if x-amz-date present) + newline + canonicalized AMZ headers (x-amz-* headers sorted, lowercased, joined with newlines) + newline + canonicalized resource (virtual-host bucket prefix + path + sorted sub-resource query params). The HMAC-SHA1 is base64-encoded and output as 'authorization=AWS <key_id>:<signature>'.

trigger_keywords: upload headers defaults
what headers are sent on object upload
copy headers metadata directive
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Upload headers defaults: content-type=application/octet-stream, x-amz-acl=private, x-amz-storage-class=STANDARD. Optional fields (cache_control, content_encoding, content_disposition) are included only when provided. Copy headers include x-amz-copy-source=<source_bucket>/<source_key>, x-amz-metadata-directive=REPLACE by default and COPY when replace=false. All outputs are sorted by header name.

trigger_keywords: object key validation
empty key error
invalid object key category
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An empty object key is rejected with error=invalid_object_key and key= (echoing the empty string). The CNAME URL form is only available when the bucket is virtual-host eligible (valid DNS label, no underscores); otherwise cname_url= is empty. Object keys are percent-encoded in URLs (spaces → %20, non-ASCII → UTF-8 percent-encoded, reserved chars like [ ] → %5B %5D).

trigger_keywords: error category format
stable error strings
language independent error codes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: All error categories must be stable, language-independent strings: 'invalid_bucket_name', 'missing_credential', 'invalid_object_key', 'remote_error'. No Python exception class names, stack traces, or runtime suffixes may appear. The field name is always 'error=' for the category and additional context fields vary by error type (name=, field=, category=, status= as appropriate).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: same escaping as that download-manager feature we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: URL percent-encoding of the HMAC-SHA1 base64 signature token, specifically encoding '+' as '%2B' and '=' as '%3D', as specified in feature2_presigned_url_signatures.json and feature3_presigned_url_generation.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: same validation philosophy as the upload flow
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The fail-fast credential validation in feature6_2_service_credential_validation.json: check both access_key_id and secret_access_key for null/missing, report missing_credential with the specific absent field name

context_pointers.vague_reference: weird edge cases with virtual-host bucket names we saw in production last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The interaction between use_virtual_host=true, use_ssl=true, and bucket name DNS-label validity described in feature7_bucket_addressing.json — SSL forces path-style regardless of virtual_host flag
x-amz-meta-* headers in request signing (feature1) must be sorted and included in the canonical AMZ headers block; in object response parsing (feature11) they are collected into a metadata map

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have the exact spec detail on that handy — can you check the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the relevant feature? The JSON files there should have the precise input/output contract. If it's about error reporting format, the rule is always: stable lowercase_underscore category strings, no runtime exception names.

feature10_1_bucket_listing_parsing.json.description: Parse a service bucket-listing document into the list of owned bucket names. Input is the raw XML body; output reports the number of buckets and their names in document order. An empty bucket set yields a count of zero and no names.

feature10_2_object_listing_parsing.json.description: Parse a bucket object-listing document into per-object attributes. Input is the raw XML body; output reports the number of objects and their keys and byte sizes in document order. An empty listing yields a count of zero; a prefixed listing yields only the matching subset.

feature10_3_location_constraint_parsing..description: Parse a bucket location-constraint document into the region value it carries. Input is the raw XML body; output reports the location code.

feature10_4_copy_result_parsing.json.description: Parse a copy-object result document into the new entity tag and last-modified timestamp of the copied object. Input is the raw XML body; output reports the entity tag exactly as carried and the last-modified value.

feature11_object_response_headers.json.description: Apply the headers and body of an object response onto the observable fields of an object handle. Content type, disposition, encoding and entity tag are taken from headers (the entity tag has its surrounding quotes stripped); custom metadata headers are collected as a name-to-values map. When a partial content-range header is present the size is taken from the range total and the body is not stored as content; otherwise the size comes from the content length and the body becomes the object content.

cases.expected_output:
content=test
content_type=image/png
content_disposition=inline
content_encoding=
size=20
etag=
metadata=x-amz-meta-test:metadata

content=
content_type=image/png
content_disposition=
content_encoding=
size=20
etag=
metadata=

feature12_http_response_handling.json.description: Interpret an HTTP response by status class. A success status passes through and reports its status code and reason phrase. A non-success status carrying an error document is mapped to a neutral remote-error result whose category is the protocol error code from the body and whose status is echoed. A non-success status with an empty or absent body maps to a generic remote-error category with the status echoed.

input.body: NoSuchBucketThe specified bucket does not exist

feature13_1_upload_request_headers.json.description: Construct the set of request headers sent when uploading an object. Defaults are applied for the access policy (private), the storage tier (STANDARD), and the content type (application/octet-stream). Optional content encoding, content disposition, and cache control are included only when provided. Output lists each header in normalized hyphenated form, sorted by name.

feature13_2_copy_request_headers.json.description: Construct the request headers sent when server-side copying an object to a new key. The copy source header is the source bucket and key joined by a slash. The metadata directive is REPLACE by default and COPY when replacement is explicitly disabled. Access policy defaults to private and content type to application/octet-stream. Output lists the copy headers in normalized hyphenated form sorted by name, followed by the destination key and the resulting object key.

cases.expected_output:
content-type=application/octet-stream
x-amz-acl=private
x-amz-copy-source=images/Lena.png
x-amz-metadata-directive=REPLACE
target_key=Lena-copy.png
result_key=Lena-copy.png

content-type=application/octet-stream
x-amz-acl=private
x-amz-copy-source=images/Lena.png
x-amz-metadata-directive=COPY
target_key=Lena-copy.png
result_key=Lena-copy.png

feature1_request_authorization_signature.description: Produce the protocol Authorization header value for a concrete HTTP request, given the request method, path, host, headers, and access credentials. The signing must respect content headers, date versus service-date headers, sub-resource query fields, virtual-host bucket addressing, repeated metadata headers, and already percent-encoded paths.

feature2_presigned_url_signatures.json.description: Produce the URL-escaped signature component used in a temporary, pre-authenticated link to an object. Input gives the bucket, the object resource path, the secret key, an absolute expiry timestamp, and optionally the HTTP method (default read), extra headers, and whether the bucket is folded into the host. Output is the single escaped signature token.

feature3_presigned_url_generation.json.description: Assemble a complete temporary object URL combining the addressing style, the access key, the expiry timestamp, and the signature query fields. When the bucket is folded into the host the bucket becomes a host prefix; otherwise it becomes a leading path segment.

feature4_query_parameter_normalization.j.description: Convert a map of request parameters into a wire-format query string. Only a fixed allow-list of listing parameters is emitted; every other key is dropped. Underscores in accepted keys become hyphens, a null value emits the bare key with no equals sign, and order follows the input order joined by ampersands.

feature5_request_header_normalization.js.description: Convert a map of request header fields into normalized HTTP header names and values. Only a fixed allow-list of content, conditional, ACL, storage-class and copy headers is kept; unrelated fields are dropped. Underscores become hyphens, null values are preserved as null, and a numeric range value is rendered as an HTTP byte range. The output lists each surviving header on its own line sorted by name, or a single empty marker line when nothing survives.

cases.expected_output:
content-disposition=inline
content-encoding=gzip
content-type=text/html
if-match=1234
if-modified-since=today
if-none-match=1243
if-unmodified-since=tomorrow
x-amz-acl=public-read
x-amz-storage-class=STANDARD

feature6_1_bucket_name_validation.json.description: Validate a storage bucket name against the naming rules before a bucket handle may be created. A name is rejected when it is empty, looks like a raw IPv4 address, is shorter than three characters, is longer than 255 characters, or begins with a dot, hyphen, or underscore. Otherwise the name is accepted as-is. Rejections are reported as a neutral validation-error category that echoes the offending name in a separate field.

feature6_2_service_credential_validation.description: Validate the credentials supplied when constructing a service client. Both an access key id and a secret access key are mandatory; if either is missing the construction is rejected with a neutral missing-credential category naming the absent field. When both are present the client is accepted.

feature7_bucket_addressing.json.description: Resolve how a bucket is addressed on the wire from its name and the transport options. Output reports the request host, the path prefix, and whether virtual-host addressing is in effect. Virtual-host addressing applies only when the name forms a valid host label and secure transport is off; otherwise the bucket is addressed through a path prefix on the shared service host.

feature8_object_addressing.json.description: Build object identifiers and links from a bucket name and an object key. The full key joins bucket and key with a slash. The standard URL uses the bucket addressing rules and percent-encodes the key (spaces, non-ASCII, and reserved characters). The CNAME URL omits the service host and is only available when the bucket is virtual-host eligible, otherwise it is empty. An empty key is rejected with a neutral invalid-key category. The address_kind field selects which form to produce.

feature9_object_policy_attributes.json.description: Normalize the access-policy, storage-tier, and cache-control attributes assigned to an object handle. An access policy symbol is canonicalized by replacing underscores with hyphens; a storage tier symbol is canonicalized to upper case; a cache-control value is stored verbatim. The selected attribute is echoed back in its normalized form.
